exercises.title: Why a base model needs SFT
exercises.prompt: A base model just predicts the next token. Explain concretely why it doesn't behave like an assistant, what SFT changes, and what 'the bridge between a token predictor and a useful assistant' means.
exercises.hint: Base model continues text — given a question it may write more questions or ramble. SFT fine-tunes on (instruction, good response) demos so the model learns the assistant pattern: answer, follow instructions, use chat format.
exercises.solution:
**Why a base model isn't an assistant:** a pre-trained base model is trained purely on **next-token prediction over web text**, so it does exactly one thing: **continue the text plausibly**. Give it 'What is the capital of France?' and it might **continue with more questions** ('What is the capital of Germany? What is the largest city...?'), because lists of questions appear in its training data — it has no notion that it's supposed to *answer*. It will ramble, fail to follow instructions, not adopt a helpful persona, and won't refuse harmful requests. It's a powerful **language model**, not a **helpful agent**.

**What SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) changes:** you fine-tune the base model on a curated dataset of **(instruction, high-quality response) demonstrations** — examples of the *behavior* you want: answering questions, following instructions, using a consistent assistant tone, formatted with **chat roles** (system/user/assistant). The objective is still next-token prediction, but now on **demonstrations of being a good assistant**, so the model **learns the input→response pattern**: 'when I see a user instruction in this format, produce a helpful answer.'

**'The bridge between a token predictor and a useful assistant':** pre-training gives the model **knowledge and language ability** (capability); SFT gives it the **behavior/format** to deploy that ability usefully (follow instructions, answer directly, stay in the assistant role). It doesn't add much new knowledge — it **redirects the existing capability** into the assistant pattern. Every chat model — Claude, GPT, Llama-Chat — goes through SFT as the **first alignment step**, and it's the cheapest, highest-impact one: a base model is unusable as a chatbot; an SFT'd model is immediately a (rough) assistant. Later steps (RLHF/DPO, Lessons 7-9) refine *which* responses are better; SFT first makes it respond at all.

exercises.title: Chat templates and loss masking
exercises.prompt: SFT data is formatted with chat templates (system/user/assistant roles) and the loss is masked so it only trains on assistant tokens. Implement the loss masking and explain why training on user/system tokens would be wrong, and why the chat template's special tokens matter.
exercises.hint: Build labels = token ids but set non-assistant positions to -100 (ignored). Training on user tokens teaches the model to generate user turns / questions, not answers. Template tokens (<|user|>, <|assistant|>) delimit roles and must be consistent + unforgeable.
exercises.solution:
**Chat template:** the conversation is serialized with **role markers**, e.g.
```
<|system|>You are a helpful assistant.<|end|>
<|user|>What is 2+2?<|end|>
<|assistant|>2 + 2 = 4.<|end|>
```
The model is trained to produce the **assistant** portion given the preceding context.

**Loss masking — only train on assistant tokens:**
```python
IGNORE = -100   # cross-entropy ignores these positions

def build_sft_labels(token_ids, role_spans):
    """role_spans: list of (start, end, role). Mask everything except assistant."""
    labels = [IGNORE] * len(token_ids)
    for start, end, role in role_spans:
        if role == 'assistant':
            for i in range(start, end):
                labels[i] = token_ids[i]      # supervise ONLY assistant tokens
    return labels
# loss = cross_entropy(logits, labels)  with labels==-100 skipped
```

**Why training on user/system tokens is wrong:** if you computed loss on the **user and system** tokens too, you'd be teaching the model to **generate user questions and system prompts** — i.e. to predict and imitate the human's side of the conversation. At inference that's useless or harmful: the model might **continue writing the user's turn**, hallucinate new questions, or blur the boundary between asking and answering. You only want it to learn **'given the conversation so far, produce the assistant response'**, so you supervise **only assistant tokens** and treat the prompt (system+user) as **fixed context** to condition on, not to predict. (The user/system tokens are still *fed in* as input — they're just not *targets*.)

**Why the template's special tokens matter:**
- **Role delimiters** (`<|user|>`, `<|assistant|>`, `<|end|>`/end-of-turn) tell the model *where each turn begins and ends* and *whose turn it is* — they're how it learns turn structure and when to stop generating.
- **Consistency:** the template at inference must **exactly match** training (same tokens, same order, same whitespace) or the model is off-distribution and quality drops — a very common real-world bug.
- **Unforgeable boundary (security):** if a **user can type a string that tokenizes to `<|assistant|>` or `<|system|>`**, they can **inject fake turns** or override the system prompt — a prompt-injection / privilege-escalation attack (Lesson 2's special-token security point). So role tokens must be **reserved, inserted only by the framework, and escaped/stripped from raw user text**. The chat template is both a **training-format contract** and a **security boundary** — getting masking or the template wrong silently corrupts the model or opens an injection hole.

exercises.title: Data quality, catastrophic forgetting, and SFT's place in the stack
exercises.prompt: SFT quality is dominated by data, not quantity. Explain why a small set of high-quality demonstrations often beats a large noisy set (the 'LIMA' insight), the risk of catastrophic forgetting / capability regression, mitigation (LoRA, mixing in pretraining data, low LR), and why SFT alone is insufficient — motivating RLHF/DPO.
exercises.hint: Quality > quantity: SFT teaches format/behavior, capability is already there; noisy demos teach bad habits. Forgetting: aggressive fine-tuning degrades base knowledge/other skills. Mitigate: LoRA, small LR, replay pretraining data. SFT can't rank responses → needs preference optimization.
exercises.solution:
**Why quality beats quantity (the LIMA insight):** SFT's job is to teach **behavior and format**, not new knowledge — the capability is already in the pretrained base. So a **small set of carefully-curated, high-quality demonstrations** (consistent style, correct answers, good formatting, diverse tasks) can produce an excellent assistant, while a **large noisy set teaches bad habits**: the model imitates whatever it sees, so sloppy, verbose, incorrect, or inconsistent demos directly become the model's behavior. LIMA ('Less Is More for Alignment') showed ~1,000 excellent examples can rival far larger datasets — because you're **eliciting and shaping** existing capability, and a few clean exemplars define the target behavior crisply. Noisy data has the opposite effect: it muddies the pattern and injects errors. The practical lesson: **curate ruthlessly; every bad demo is actively harmful.**

**Catastrophic forgetting / capability regression:** fine-tuning **moves the weights**, and if you push too hard (high LR, too many epochs, narrow data) the model **overfits to the SFT distribution and degrades** its broad pretrained abilities — it may get worse at coding, reasoning, or languages **not represented in the SFT set**, become repetitive, or lose calibration. You traded generality for the narrow SFT behavior.
**Mitigations:**
- **Low learning rate + few epochs:** nudge the weights gently; SFT should be a light touch, not a retraining.
- **LoRA / parameter-efficient fine-tuning (Phase 8.8):** train small low-rank adapters instead of all weights — the base stays frozen, so it **can't catastrophically forget**, and adapters are cheap/swappable.
- **Mix in diverse / pretraining-like data** (replay) and **keep the SFT set broad** so you don't narrow the distribution.
- **Regularize toward the base** (KL/weight penalties) and monitor a **broad eval suite** (Lesson 10) for regressions, not just instruction-following.

**Why SFT alone is insufficient → RLHF/DPO:** SFT teaches the model to **imitate good responses**, but it has a fundamental limit: it only ever sees **one 'correct' demonstration per prompt** and trains the model to **mimic** it. It **can't express that response A is *better* than response B** when both are plausible — there's no notion of *ranking* or *preference*, and many qualities (helpfulness, harmlessness, tone, avoiding confident errors) are about **relative** quality and about **what NOT to do**, which demonstrations can't easily convey. SFT also can't easily teach the model to **refuse** well or to optimize for human-judged quality beyond imitation. That's exactly the gap **preference optimization** fills: **RLHF (Lesson 7)** trains a reward model on human **comparisons** and optimizes the policy toward higher-rated responses; **DPO (Lesson 8)** does the same directly from preference pairs. So the canonical recipe is **SFT first (learn to respond / the format), then RLHF/DPO (learn which responses are better)** — SFT gets the model into the right behavioral neighborhood cheaply, and preference methods refine *quality* within it. SFT is necessary but not sufficient: it makes an assistant; alignment makes a *good* one.
